companyCaseStudy.title: How Instagram Tamed Feed Fan‑Out at 500M Users — Cutting Write Amplification by ~99% with a Hybrid Timeline
companyCaseStudy.summary: When Instagram climbed to hundreds of millions of users, a single popular post could trigger millions of writes. Instagram solved the 'fan‑out' problem with a hybrid push/pull timeline, sharded in‑memory timelines, and async workers — reducing write amplification dramatically and keeping read latency in the low tens of milliseconds.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook — a single post, millions of writes

A celebrity posts a photo. They have 1,000,000 followers. Naively pushing that photo into every follower's timeline means 1,000,000 writes just for one post — repeated thousands of times per hour. Instagram hit this exact scale as it grew into the hundreds of millions of users and had to keep the home timeline fast and fresh while avoiding mountains of duplicated work and overloaded DBs.

### Problem: write amplification + skewed follow graph

- Scale: Instagram crossed hundreds of millions of monthly active users (500M+ by mid‑2016).  
- Workload: a tiny fraction of creators (celebrities) produced posts that should be visible to millions — extreme skew in follower counts.  
- Naive approach: fan‑out‑on‑write (push every update into every follower's timeline) => simple reads, but write amplification grew proportionally to follower counts and overloaded stores.

Why this matters: 1 post x 1M followers = 1M writes. At thousands of posts/hour you get billions of writes, huge storage duplication, and replication churn.

### Challenge: keep read latency low without exploding writes or storage

Requirements Instagram set out to meet:
- Home timeline reads: sub‑100ms for 95th percentile.  
- Freshness: new posts should appear within seconds.  
- Cost and operational limits: avoid generating and storing millions of duplicated timeline objects for every celebrity post.

Tradeoffs:
- Fan‑out‑on‑write: great reads, huge writes/storage.  
- Fan‑out‑on‑read: fewer writes, but reads become expensive (merge many sources on the fly, higher latency) and can cause inconsistent UX for typical users.

### Solution: a hybrid push/pull timeline + sharded in‑memory timelines

Instagram implemented a pragmatic hybrid:
- For "normal" users (small follower counts), do fan‑out‑on‑write: push new post IDs into follower timelines stored in highly available in‑memory shards (Redis/memcached tiers). This keeps reads cheap for the majority of users.  
- For "superstar" accounts with very large follower sets (threshold chosen experimentally, e.g. >100k), do fan‑out‑on‑read: don't push to millions of timelines at write time. Instead, mark the post in a lightweight store and have reads merge that creator's recent posts into the follower's timeline on demand.
- Timelines are partitioned/sharded by user ID across an in‑memory cluster (Redis or similar). Background workers (asynchronous task queue) perform writes into those timeline shards and compact/purge old entries.
- Backfill / rehydrate: if a follower hasn't been active for a while, on next read the system will fetch missing items from the canonical store (Postgres) and populate the in‑memory timeline shard lazily.

Benefits:
- Small followers: still get precomputed timelines and very cheap reads.  
- Large followers/celeb posts: avoids millions of writes at post time; reads pay a small additional merge cost only when needed.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  UserClient --> API[API Servers]
  API --> Auth[Auth / Rate Limit]
  API --> TimelineService[Timeline Service]
  TimelineService --> TimelineCache[Sharded Timeline Cache (Redis shards)]
  TimelineService --> PullStore[Per‑creator store (recent posts)]
  PostCreate --> WriteRouter[Write Router]
  WriteRouter -->|followers < threshold| FanoutWorkers[Fan‑out Workers]
  WriteRouter -->|followers >= threshold| MarkStore[Superstar Markstore]
  FanoutWorkers --> TimelineCache
  PullStore --> TimelineService
  MarkStore --> TimelineService
```

### Before / After (representative numbers)

| Metric | Before (naive fan‑out) | After (hybrid) |
|--------|------------------------|----------------|
| Worst‑case writes per celebrity post | ~1,000,000 | ~0 (deferred, marker only) |
| Average writes per post (across all posts) | 100s -> 1,000s | 10s -> 100s |
| Timeline read p95 latency | 200–500 ms (merge heavy) | 30–80 ms |
| Storage for duplicated timeline entries | Massive (billions of entries) | Reduced by ~80–99% for high‑fanout posts |
| DB load (write QPS) | Spiky, high peaks | Smoother — write QPS dropped by ~50–90% in hotspots |

> Note: Instagram tuned the exact thresholds and cache sizes empirically. The hybrid approach turns multi‑million write spikes into a small number of cheap operations and a read‑side merge for infrequent accesses.

### Implementation details & operational choices

- Sharding: timeline cache sharded by follower user id (consistent hashing / range sharding) so writes/reads for a user go to a small subset of nodes.  
- Background workers: asynchronous task queue (workers that can fan out in batches) decouple user request latency from heavy write work; they also implement batching and write coalescing.  
- Thresholds: a follower count threshold determines push vs pull; thresholds are revisited periodically to react to changing access patterns.  
- Hot‑key handling: superstar creators are tracked as hot keys. The system keeps a small recent window of their posts in a compact PullStore for fast on‑the‑fly merging.  
- Backpressure & retries: if fan‑out workers fall behind, the system serves a best effort timeline combining cached items and on‑the‑fly reads while replaying the rest asynchronously.

### Results (impact)

- Write amplification for giant fanouts dropped by an order of magnitude (often 95–99%) because superstar posts stopped generating millions of writes.  
- Tail read latency improved from hundreds of milliseconds to tens of milliseconds for the majority of users because timelines remained precomputed for the common case.  
- Storage and replication costs fell substantially — avoiding billions of redundant timeline rows saved money and simplified replica catchup.

### Why this worked

- Real workloads are skewed: optimize for the common case (normal users) but handle skewed cases differently.  
- Hybrid approaches let you get the best of both worlds — low read latency for most users, and economically manageable writes for rare heavy events.  
- Operational controls (thresholds, monitoring of hot keys, asynchronous workers) make the system adaptive and debuggable.

### Tradeoffs and gotchas

- Complexity: hybrid systems are more complex than pure push or pure pull — more code paths and operational modes.  
- Cold reads: users who were offline may see slightly slower first reads as their timeline is backfilled.  
- Threshold tuning: picking a threshold that matches traffic patterns is an ongoing operational task.

### Visual summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  P[Post Created] --> R{FollowerCount < THRESHOLD?}
  R -- yes --> W[Fan‑out Workers]
  W --> T[Write to Sharded Timeline Cache]
  R -- no --> M[Mark Post in PullStore]
  M --> T2[TimelineService will merge on read]
  User --> API[Read Timeline]
  API -->|precomputed present| T
  API -->|needs merge| PullStore
```
keyTakeaways: Use a hybrid push/pull strategy to handle extreme follower skew — push for the common case, pull for superstars to avoid write storms.
Shard timeline caches by user id and keep a small per‑creator recent store for on‑the‑fly merges; this preserves fast reads while reducing storage and write QPS.
Tune thresholds and operate hot‑key tracking + async workers — practical engineering, monitoring, and staged rollouts make the hybrid approach robust.
relatedTopics: Caching & CDN strategies for read-heavy workloads

concept.title: Write Every Change, Read the World: Practical Event Sourcing + CQRS
concept.summary: Event Sourcing with CQRS turns state changes into an immutable timeline (events) and separates write models from read models, making audits, temporal queries, and complex business logic easier to reason about. This post explains why that matters, how the pieces fit together, and practical pitfalls to avoid when adopting it.
concept.explanation:
## Why this matters — the real-world problem

Imagine trying to debug why an invoice's amount changed three weeks ago, or roll back a partial migration, or reconstruct a user's activity for analytics. With a single mutable row that gets overwritten on every update, you lose intent, sequence, and the "why" behind state.

Event Sourcing stores *every change* as an append-only event (the facts). CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) splits how you *write* (commands -> events) from how you *read* (projections/queries). Together they give you an audit log, time-travel, easier complex workflows, and flexible read models for performance.

### Analogy: Bank ledger vs. spreadsheet

- Traditional CRUD is like editing the balance cell in a spreadsheet — you only see the latest number.
- Event Sourcing is like a bank ledger: every debit/credit is recorded in order. You can recompute the balance, audit, or replay history.

### Core concepts (short)

- Event: a fact that something happened (e.g., AccountCredited { accountId, amount, time }).
- Event Store: append-only store of events (source of truth).
- Aggregate: domain object that validates commands and emits events.
- Projection / Read Model: materialized view built by processing events (optimized for queries).
- CQRS: separate code paths and storage for command (writes) and query (reads).

### How it fits together (sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>CommandHandler: Submit Command (CreditAccount)
  CommandHandler->>Aggregate: Load state (replay events)
  Aggregate-->>CommandHandler: Validate & emit Events
  CommandHandler->>EventStore: Append Events
  EventStore-->>Projections: Push Event (async)
  Projections-->>QueryDB: Update Read Models
  Client->>QueryAPI: Query Read Model (GetBalance)
  QueryAPI-->>QueryDB: Read optimized view
  QueryDB-->>Client: Return Balance
```

### Before / After (CRUD vs Event Sourcing + CQRS)

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| CRUD (single-state DB row) | Simple, easy to implement | Loses history, hard to audit, difficult to reconstruct past state | Simple apps, low regulatory needs, few complex invariants |
| Event Sourcing + CQRS | Immutable audit log, replayable, temporal queries, flexible read models, better for complex domain logic | More moving parts, eventual consistency, harder debugging at first, migration complexity | Financial systems, audit-heavy apps, complex domain logic, analytics-heavy apps |

### Visual flow (high-level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  CMD[Client Command] --> AH[Command Handler]
  AH --> AG[Aggregate (replay events)]
  AG --> EV[Emit Event]
  EV --> ES[Event Store (append-only)]
  ES --> PR[Projections / Read Models]
  PR --> API[Query API]
  API --> CLIENT[Client reads]
```

### Minimal example (Node-style pseudocode)

```javascript
// Append an event to the store
async function appendEvent(streamId, event) {
  await eventStore.append(streamId, event) // idempotent append with expected version
}

// Command handler
async function handleCreditAccount(cmd) {
  const events = await eventStore.load(cmd.accountId)
  const state = rebuildAccount(events)
  if (cmd.amount <= 0) throw new Error('invalid amount')
  const evt = { type: 'AccountCredited', amount: cmd.amount, time: Date.now() }
  await appendEvent(cmd.accountId, evt)
  // publish for projections (could be same store tailing mechanism)
  await publisher.publish(evt)
}

// Simple projection (subscribe to events)
publisher.on('AccountCredited', async evt => {
  await readDb.incrementBalance(evt.accountId, evt.amount)
})
```

### When to use Event Sourcing + CQRS

- You need a full audit trail or legal compliance (payments, trading).
- You want *temporal queries* (what did the state look like at T?) or to replay history for new features.
- Your domain logic is complex and benefits from an explicit sequence of domain events.
- You have read models that need to be optimized differently than write paths (highly read-heavy APIs).

### Common pitfalls & how to avoid them

- *Overengineering*: Not every app needs it. Start with CRUD, migrate to events when you actually need history or complex workflows.
- *Event schema drift*: Events are immutable; design versioning strategies (schema version field, upcasters, or transform during replay).
- *Event granularity*: Too coarse = poor reusability; too fine = noisy event store. Aim for domain-significant facts.
- *Consistency confusion*: Reads are usually eventually consistent. Surface this to clients (stale reads, read-your-writes). Use request-level caching or read-your-own-write tokens when necessary.
- *Rebuild cost*: Replaying every event to rebuild aggregates can be slow. Use snapshots periodically to speed up load.
- *Operational complexity*: Event stores, projection pipelines, idempotency, and duplicate events add operational overhead. Invest in tooling and observability.

### Practical tips

- Start with a small bounded context rather than converting your entire system at once.
- Keep events backward-compatible. Add metadata (version, correlationId, commandId).
- Make event handling idempotent: dedupe using event IDs or expected versioning.
- Use snapshots for hot aggregates that have long event histories.
- Consider hybrid: write events to store but also update the transactional DB for the canonical write model if you need stronger consistency for certain operations.

### Failure modes to watch

- Projection lag causing user-facing stale data.
- Unhandled exceptions during projection causing gaps in read models — build retry and DLQ for projections.
- Concurrent commands against the same aggregate — use optimistic concurrency (expected version) or serialize commands at aggregate level.

### Quick checklist before adopting

- Do you need an audit trail or temporal queries? If no, don't adopt yet.
- Can your team handle operational complexity? If not, design simpler first.
- Have you defined event schemas and versioning rules? If not, do that up front.

### Closing thought

Event Sourcing + CQRS swaps some simplicity for powerful capabilities: an accurate history of truth and flexible, performant reads. When used for the right problems (auditability, complex domains, multi-read-model needs), it becomes a force multiplier — but it requires discipline around schema, idempotency, and eventual consistency.
keyTakeaways: Event Sourcing records every state change as an immutable event — the event log is the source of truth.
CQRS separates writes (commands/events) from reads (projections), enabling optimized read models and scale.
Watch out for eventual consistency, schema versioning, idempotency, and replay/snapshot strategies — start small and instrument heavily.
